sources.notes: Hard version of constructing a long sequence with pairwise distinct adjacent gcds.
Official solution constructs numbers from prime powers and precomputes long Eulerian-style walks with enough unique adjacent gcds.

annotation.summary: Construct a long sequence of integers whose adjacent gcds are all different.
annotation.constraints: The hard version needs a scalable construction for large n under integer bounds, so the set of base numbers and walk length must be precomputed carefully.
annotation.core_idea: Build a grid of numbers from independent prime powers so the gcd of two chosen vertices is determined by their pair of indices/edge. Precompute a family of walks whose consecutive pairs cover many distinct edges without repetition. Pick the smallest walk long enough for n and output the corresponding numbers; because no edge is repeated, all adjacent gcds are distinct.
annotation.complexity: O(K^2 + n) precomputation/output, with small fixed K around 100 in the editorial implementation.
tricks: encode edge identity into gcd by prime exponents
choose the shortest sufficient walk length

solution_variants.name: prime-grid-euler-walk
solution_variants.summary: Generate a fixed set of prime-power numbers and use a precomputed Eulerian-style walk over their indices so each adjacent gcd is unique.

solution_variants.name: randomized-prime-set
solution_variants.summary: A community variant chooses random prime-subsets for vertices; with enough primes, adjacent gcd collisions can be avoided with high probability.

tags.evidence: The hard version is an explicit sequence construction using a designed finite object.
The sequence is obtained from a walk that avoids repeated edges, which is the mechanism that keeps adjacent gcds distinct.
tags.description: Euler trail/path style constructions or checks where edges must be used exactly once or at most once in a graph walk.
tags.created_reason: Euler-walk constructions are a standard graph technique and useful to retrieve distinct-edge/gcd construction problems.

tags.evidence: Prime exponents are arranged so each traversed edge has a distinct gcd value.
The construction depends on counting and recursively extending a walk family with enough distinct adjacent pairs.
